title: The Accuracy Trap
concept: Raw accuracy can be highly misleading when data is imbalanced, making a model seem correct when it just guesses the majority class.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main characteristic of the probabilities output by a Softmax function?

options.text: They always sum to exactly 1.0 (or 100%) across all classes
They are independent and can each be greater than 1.0
They can be negative if the model has low confidence

exercises.explain: Correct! Softmax converts raw scores into probabilities that sum to exactly 1.0, representing the model's relative confidence for each class.

cards.title: The 99% accurate doctor
cards.body: Imagine a clinic where only 1% of patients have a rare condition. A lazy doctor decides to diagnose every single patient as 'healthy' without even looking at them. How accurate is this doctor?

cards.title: Imbalanced data
cards.body: Imbalanced data occurs when one class is far more common than the other. When 99% of your data belongs to one category, raw accuracy (percentage of correct guesses) becomes a trap.

cards.title: The broken weather app
cards.body: Think of an app in the Sahara Desert that always predicts 'sunny'. Since it rarely rains, the app has 99.9% accuracy. But it is completely useless for predicting the rare event of rain!

cards.title: Spotting credit card fraud
cards.body: Out of 10,000 credit card charges, maybe only 10 are fraudulent. A model that flags zero transactions as fraud will achieve 99.9% accuracy, but it fails to catch a single thief.

cards.title: Myth: High accuracy means a great model
cards.body: False. A model can have 99% accuracy but be completely blind to the rare events we actually want to detect. We need better metrics to measure real performance.

cards.title: The accuracy illusion
cards.body: Why raw accuracy fails on imbalanced datasets:

left.text:
50% positive, 50% negative.
Accuracy is a reliable metric.

right.text:
1% positive, 99% negative.
99% accuracy is easy by just guessing 'negative'.

exercises.prompt: Why is raw accuracy a poor metric when evaluating a model trained to detect credit card fraud?

options.text: Fraud is extremely rare, so a model can get high accuracy by just guessing 'no fraud'
Accuracy is too slow to calculate in real time
Fraudulent transactions are always larger than normal transactions
Accuracy only works for regression models, not classification

exercises.explain: Since fraud is rare, a model that does nothing but guess 'no fraud' achieves near-perfect accuracy while being completely useless.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model with 99% accuracy on imbalanced data is guaranteed to perform well in the real world.
exercises.explain: False. High accuracy on imbalanced data often just means the model is good at guessing the common class while missing all rare events.

exercises.sentence: When one class is much more common than the other, we call this ___ data.
exercises.explain: Imbalanced datasets have a large skew toward one class, making standard accuracy misleading.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a classic example of the accuracy trap?

options.text: An email filter that achieves 95% accuracy because 95% of incoming emails are spam, so it just marks everything as spam
A model predicting house prices that is off by an average of $5,000 per home
A system classifying images of cats and dogs where both classes have 500 images

exercises.explain: When the model gets high accuracy simply by predicting the dominant class and ignoring the rare one, it's trapped!

exercises.prompt: You build an AI model to predict rare engine failures that happen in 0.1% of flights. The model simply predicts 'no failure' for every flight and gets 99.9% accuracy. What is the best conclusion?

options.text: The model is dangerous and useless because it fails to catch any failures, despite high accuracy.
The model is ready to deploy because 99.9% accuracy is extremely high.
The dataset is too small to make any conclusions.
The model is stable and should be retrained with more common classes.

exercises.explain: Exactly! An engine failure model that predicts 'no failure' does not keep anyone safe. Accuracy alone cannot evaluate this system. 🎉
